denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have completed our review of the prior authorization request your provider submitted for inpatient medical detoxification (a 3 to 5 day medically managed withdrawal admission) to treat alcohol use disorder. After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin and the InterQual behavioral health subset, this request is not authorized. Determination date: May 19, 2026.

Aetna's behavioral health coverage directs that a less costly, clinically appropriate modality be used before an inpatient admission is approved. Our reviewer concluded that ambulatory (outpatient) detoxification is the appropriate setting for managed withdrawal and that the submitted records do not establish that outpatient detoxification would be inadequate. The level-of-care criteria applied here are those adopted in Aetna's 2020 Behavioral Health Medical Policy. Separately, your plan contains a plan exclusion for inpatient and residential substance-use detoxification services, which independently applies to this request. Inpatient medical detoxification is therefore denied; outpatient detoxification remains available as a covered modality.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific clinical criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative may submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, together with any additional clinical information. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal is completed.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management
clinical_context: The member is a 49-year-old man with severe alcohol use disorder and a history of complicated alcohol withdrawal. On the most recent presentation his CIWA-Ar peaked at 18 with tremor, diaphoresis, and a heart rate of 112; his blood alcohol concentration on arrival was 0.31, and a prior withdrawal two years earlier was complicated by a generalized seizure. GGT was elevated at 210 U/L. The evaluating emergency physician judged ambulatory detoxification unsafe given his withdrawal severity and seizure history and recommended a 3 to 5 day medically managed inpatient withdrawal. The treating addiction specialist documents that current ASAM 4th edition criteria support medically managed inpatient withdrawal for patients with high CIWA-Ar scores and prior complicated withdrawal, superseding the older policy the insurer applied. The member is covered under a fully-insured plan issued in his state, where the substance-use-disorder parity mandate requires withdrawal-management benefits at parity with medical care; that state mandate preempts the plan exclusion cited in the denial. Outpatient detoxification has previously failed to maintain a safe withdrawal and is not appropriate for the current risk.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Aetna, Prior Authorization, behavioral_health, 41-55, M, modality_substitution). F10.20 belongs to behavioral_health; inpatient detox realistic. Rationale enacts modality_substitution (directs to ambulatory detox). Age band not 71+, so null employer_archetype fine.
evidence_quotes: "sub_tactic": "modality_substitution"
directs the member to ambulatory (outpatient) detoxification

scenario_realism.reasoning: Highly plausible on a commercial Aetna plan. ASAM-derived step-down logic and hand-wavy 'outpatient not shown inadequate' mirror real denials. Exploitable gaps clinically credible (CIWA-Ar 17, prior complicated withdrawal contraindicate outpatient). superseded_guideline and parity/plan-exclusion conflict for a fully-insured plan are real levers. Coherent dx/tx/demographics.
evidence_quotes: it directs the member to ambulatory (outpatient) detoxification and states that inpatient medical detoxification is not authorized because the lower-cost modality has not been shown to be inadequate
Because the plan is fully-insured, the state's substance-use-disorder parity mandate preempts the plan exclusion

insurer_voice.reasoning: Hits Aetna's two strongest fingerprints (Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin + InterQual) and avoids competitor tells (no MCG/Milliman, no Optum). Cool procedural cadence matching Aetna house style. Minor step-therapy framing leans faintly Cigna but the dominant correct tells would convince a claims handler.
evidence_quotes: After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin and the InterQual behavioral health subset, this request is not authorized.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management

denial_logic.reasoning: Cites real plausible guidelines (Aetna CPB, InterQual BH subset, 2020 policy) — not hallucinated. Logic authentically shoddy and exploitable: asserts a less costly modality without a named threshold, near-circular burden-shift ('records do not establish outpatient would be inadequate'), bolted-on plan exclusion. Leaves ample appeal room.
evidence_quotes: a less costly, clinically appropriate modality be used before an inpatient admission is approved
the submitted records do not establish that outpatient detoxification would be inadequate

clinical_realism.reasoning: Internally coherent and credible: CIWA-Ar 18 with tremor/diaphoresis/HR 112, BAC 0.31 with active withdrawal (high tolerance), prior withdrawal seizure elevating risk, GGT 210 U/L realistic, 3-5 day medically managed withdrawal standard. No fabricated drugs or impossible courses.

a prior withdrawal two years earlier was complicated by a generalized seizure
GGT was elevated at 210 U/L

Inpatient medical detoxification (3-5 day medically managed withdrawal)

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Two independently fatal weaknesses: outdated 2020 policy vs current ASAM 4th edition supporting inpatient withdrawal for high CIWA-Ar + prior complicated withdrawal; and a plan exclusion preempted by the state SUD parity mandate on a fully-insured plan. The denial's premise that records do not establish outpatient inadequacy is contradicted by CIWA-Ar 18, HR 112, BAC 0.31, prior withdrawal seizure, and an ED physician judging ambulatory detox unsafe.
exploitable_weaknesses: Insurer applied the outdated 2020 Behavioral Health Medical Policy when current ASAM 4th edition criteria support inpatient managed withdrawal
Plan exclusion for inpatient detox is preempted by the state substance-use-disorder parity mandate on this fully-insured plan
Denial's premise that records do not establish outpatient inadequacy is contradicted by documented withdrawal severity and ED physician judgment
strong_defenses: Procedural rights are properly disclosed: 180-day appeal window, external review availability, peer-to-peer offer, free copy of clinical criteria

current ASAM 4th edition criteria support medically managed inpatient withdrawal ... superseding the older policy the insurer applied
that state mandate preempts the plan exclusion cited in the denial
